name: Melide - tour around Pena da Mouro and Arzúa
name_original: Melide - vuelta por al Pena da Mouro y Arzúa
region_intro: Road cycling around Melide, situated in the Lugano Region of Switzerland, offers diverse landscapes and natural features. The area is characterized by its location on Lake Lugano, featuring the Melide causeway with a dedicated cycle path. Cyclists encounter rolling routes, valleys, and panoramic roads that traverse vineyards and provide views of the surrounding mountains. The region's varied terrain includes both moderate lakeside loops and challenging routes with significant elevation gains through mountain passes. Best road cycling routes around Melide The most popular road cycling route is Arzua - return via Embalse de Portodemouros, a 31.2 miles (50.3 km) trail that takes 2 hours 40 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation changes through varied terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Melide - tour around Pena da Mouro and Arzúa, a difficult 40.1 miles (64.5 km) path. This route winds through rural landscapes with notable climbs and descents. Local road cyclists also love the Sobrado Lagoon – Lake Sobrado dos Monxes loop from Arzúa, a 45.4 miles (73.1 km) trail leading through picturesque lake and lagoon areas, often completed in about 3 hours 24 minutes. Road cycling around Melide is defined by lakeside paths, panoramic roads, and routes through vineyards and valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to more challenging climbs. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 265 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Melide's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of road cycling routes can I find around Melide?
A: Melide offers a diverse range of road cycling routes. You'll find moderate lakeside loops, such as those around Lake Lugano, as well as more challenging rides with significant elevation gains through mountain passes, vineyards, and valleys. The region is known for its panoramic roads and the unique Melide causeway, which features a dedicated cycle path across Lake Lugano.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Melide?
A: There are over 45 road cycling routes around Melide listed on komoot, catering to various skill levels. This includes 1 easy route, 25 moderate routes, and 19 difficult routes.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Melide?
A: Yes, Melide offers easy road cycling options, particularly along the shores of Lake Lugano. The Melide causeway provides a relatively flat and scenic ride across the lake, ideal for those seeking a less strenuous experience. While most routes are moderate to difficult, there is at least one easy route available.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Experienced riders looking for a challenge can explore routes with significant elevation changes. Examples include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e969932645/arzua-return-via-embalse-de-portodemouros" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arzua - return via Embalse de Portodemouros&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 50.3 km trail with over 900 meters of elevation gain, or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e969964220/melide-tour-around-pena-da-mouro-and-arzua" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Melide - tour around Pena da Mouro and Arzúa&lt;/a&gt;, a 64.5 km path winding through rural landscapes with notable climbs and descents.

Q: What natural features and landmarks can I see along road cycling routes in Melide?
A: Road cycling routes around Melide offer stunning views of Lake Lugano and the surrounding mountains. You can also encounter various natural monuments and historical sites. Notable attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/359371" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sobrado Lagoon&lt;/a&gt;, and historical bridges like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/137014" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ribadiso Medieval Bridge&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7126090" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Bridge of Furelos&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes also pass through picturesque vineyards and valleys.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes around Melide?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Melide are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20158800" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sobrado Lagoon – Lake Sobrado dos Monxes loop from Arzúa&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through picturesque lake and lagoon areas.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Melide?
A: The Lugano region, including Melide, is generally pleasant for road cycling from spring through autumn. The mild climate allows for comfortable rides, especially along the lake. Summer can be warm, but lakeside routes often offer refreshing breezes. Always check local weather forecasts before heading out.

Q: Is parking available for cyclists in Melide?
A: Melide, being a popular destination, typically offers various parking options. Cyclists can usually find public parking facilities in and around the town center or near popular starting points for routes. It's advisable to check specific parking availability for your chosen starting location.

Q: Can I access road cycling routes in Melide using public transport?
A: Switzerland has an excellent public transport network. Melide is well-connected, and it's often possible to reach starting points of cycling routes by train or bus. Many public transport services allow bicycles, though specific rules and peak hour restrictions may apply. Check with local transport providers for details on bike carriage.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling options in Melide?
A: For families, the relatively flat and dedicated cycle path along the Melide causeway is an excellent option. While many routes involve significant climbs, the lakeside areas provide gentler terrain suitable for family outings. Consider shorter, less challenging loops for a more relaxed experience.

Q: Where can I find cafes or restaurants along the cycling routes?
A: Many road cycling routes around Melide pass through charming villages and towns where you can find cafes, restaurants, and local eateries. The cross-border nature of the Lake Lugano area means you can enjoy varied culinary experiences in both Swiss and Italian villages. The main towns like Melide and Lugano offer numerous options for refreshments.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Melide?
A: The road cycling routes around Melide are highly regarded by the komoot community, with over 265 road cyclists having explored the varied terrain. Riders often praise the breathtaking lakeside views, the unique experience of cycling across the Melide causeway, and the diverse landscapes ranging from flat paths to challenging mountain climbs through vineyards and valleys.
tips:
A section of the Camino de Santiago do Norte enters through Arzúa to cross the so-called Monta da Mota (460 m) where on its soft top sits the hermitage or chapel of San Bartolomé de A Mota (Santo Estevo de Campo) that the French author Jean Pierre Racq already named from his "Guide for pilgrims to Santiago de Compostela" back in the late eighteenth century (1790).
A "mota", apart from certain meanings in Latin America, is a low elevation that rises on a plain, nothing better to define this place. Sometimes the "specks" are also related to old defensive sites although here we do not have information about any. What we do know is the presence in the parish and in the surroundings of several millenary "mamoas", so it is possible that some of them were the cause of the origin of this hermitage of miraculous saints.
This chapel is hidden under a "carballeira" (oak forest) that seems to have been planted by the neighbors themselves. The story went that if a "carballo" burned, the son of the one who had planted it did not fulfill the military service.
The chapel is dedicated to Saints Bartholomew, Matthew and Saint Simon and on them three pilgrimages are held throughout the year: September 21 (San Mateo); Saint Bartholomew (August 24) and Saint Simon (October 28).
In the one of San Bartolomé they say that the goiter can be cured if the priest passes the sword of the saint down your throat.
Inside we have a baroque altarpiece of the XVIII with images of San Pedro, Asunción and San Pablo. The current chapel is also from the same century and has a rectangular plan with a sacristy and a portico added on its north side. Its sober white façade only shows a linteled door, a flared window above it and a belfry with double bell tower topped with two pinnacles on the sides. In the esplanade that surrounds the hermitage there is a simple cruceiro of the same period and a fountain.
https://www.galiciamaxica.eu/galicia/a-coruna/comarca-de-arzua/arzua/ermitadamota/
Quiet road of 9 km from the Carretera DP 0604 direction Arzúa

In Arzúa, the pilgrimage routes of the Camino Francés cross with those of the Camino del Norte. You will find many accommodation options here, but there will also be significantly more traffic on the Camino de Santiago from now on.

The A Saleta fountain has become a symbol of the pilgrim and many people stop at this point to cool off.
Boente is a small town where around 100 people live and which has several accommodations and a cafeteria.
Regional municipality of Camino de Santiago and EV routes (EuroVelo). Great midway place to eat and fill your bottles for the return trip
